title: practical english usage - Part 331
content:
Learn is often irregular in British English (learn/learnt) and normally regular in 1  forms 508 le  arn   _________________________ I'm going to lay down for a few minutes. (Standard English ... lie down ...) used. Lay is often used in cases where standard English has lie.

In many British and American dialects, different forms of lay and irregular lie are 4 dialect     forms You lied to me when you said you loved me.

The  regular verb lie (lied) means 'say things that are not true'.

I lay down and closed my eyes, (     I laid down-. ..) not Don't lie in bed all day. Get up and do some work, (     Don't lay-...) Lie (irregular) means 'be down', 'be/become horizontal'. It has no object.

-ing form: lying   past participle: lain (used mostly in a formal/literary style) infinitive: (to) lie   past: lay The  forms of the irregular verb lie are: 2 lie   (irregular) lay an egg (a bird's way of having a baby).

Note the expressions lay a table (= put plates, knives, etc on a table) and not I laid the papers on the table and sat down, (     L4ay-...) Lay the tent down on the grass and I'll see how to put it up.

Lay means 'put down carefully' or 'put down flat’ . It has an object.

-ing form: laying        past: laid infinitive: (to) lay       past participle: laid Lay is a regular verb except for its spelling. Its forms are: 1  lay spelling), lie (irregular) and lie (regular).

There are three similar verbs that can be confused: lay (regular except for 507 lay    and   lie Word  Problems from A  to Z      Section 31 509 vocabulary' •         left not She didn't let me see what she was doing, (     ... let me saw.. .) rt/ ɔtteʃ /  ɔeetʃtrrg . •  • ) izʌ r 4 si II / ɪfZXUl JAXI-     I not We usually let the children stay up late on Saturdays, (     ... let the children object           + infinitive               without Let is followed by                                            to.

1 followed       by   infinitive       without      to 512  let                       ______ The government must act, lest the problem of child poverty grow worse.

Lest can be followed by a subjunctive verb (►  232).

We must take care lest evil thoughts enter our hearts.

They kept watch all night lest robbers should come.

language. It is a little more common in formal American English.

in British English, and is found mostly in older literature and in ceremonial Lest has a similar meaning to in case (►  248) or so that... not (►  588). It is rare 511  lest       ________________ the lesser of two evils  a lesser-known writer or ‘ not so much'.

Lesser is used in a few expressions (in a rather formal style) to mean ‘ smaller’ 510 lesser ►    408 and lend        borrow I haven't got any money left: can you get the tickets?

There's nothing left in the fridge.

Left is common after there is and have got.

After the explosion, only two people were left alive.

What did you do with the money that was left?

‘ remaining’ , ‘ not used’ , ‘ still there’ .

The  past participle of leave - left - can be used in a special way, to mean 509 left . 4711 and leave         forget In the new job, I soon learnt to keep my mouth  shut.

Children usually learn to walk at around one year old.

we  generally use learn to . . ..

When  we  talk about less conscious skills and other kinds of knowledge, vocabulary •  514  like: verb not 'What do you think of the soup? *1 like it.’ (     . . .-I-m liking it.) Like is not usually used in progressive forms (►  4).

1 not   used    in  progressive         forms 514  like:   verb For more about countable and uncountable nouns, ►JIA.

My mother’s parents lived interesting lives.

My grandmother had a hard life, (     ... had hard life.) When  we  describe particular people's lives, life is normally countable.

I think I would enjoy city life, (     ... a city life.) Life is complicated. Anna enjoys life.

uncountable.
